nodes.label: SCFF cheap brain final architecture (v1.0.0)
nodes.rationale: Ideal-math design of the cheap forward-only half, frozen across five phases; the pre-noise base.

nodes.label: Windowed two-view InfoNCE objective
nodes.rationale: The committed SCFF objective: two randomly-masked views of a flat vector, run siamese through shared weights over a w-layer window, pulled together under InfoNCE. Replaces energy-goodness with information preserved about the class DIRECTION; contribution = making local InfoNCE work on structureless flat vectors.

nodes.label: Coordination window w (blocked-backprop truncation)
nodes.rationale: Credit flows within w adjacent layers, cut across windows (committed w=2/stride 2; w=4 depth-closer; w=12 = a forbidden full backward pass, the ceiling). The bounded chain is why backward compute stays flat in depth.

nodes.label: Sharper InfoNCE temperature tau=0.2 (depth cure)
nodes.rationale: A sharper temperature concentrates each update on the hardest negatives, making it class-selective; ~82% of the lift survives an lr-match, so it is mostly DIRECTION not step size. Cures the depth-decay (objective-locality, not an intrinsic Tunnel).

nodes.label: Mandatory per-sample L2 norm
nodes.rationale: Load-bearing twice: forbids a layer cheating by inflating ‖h‖, and being per-sample (no running stats) makes the cell continual-friendly and nuisance-robust — but is ALSO the source of the A7 directional-noise sensitivity.

nodes.label: CLAPP / Greedy InfoMax (local InfoNCE)
nodes.rationale: Forward-only gradient-isolated modules trained with local InfoNCE whose representations improve layer over layer — the existence proof that the Phase-2 wall was the objective, not locality. CLAPP is the single-sample/online form that streams the negatives on-chip.

nodes.label: SimCLR positive pair
nodes.rationale: Augmentation-based two-view positive under InfoNCE; ours uses random coordinate-masking on flat vectors inside a local window. Committed cell = SimCLR's positive, CLAPP's locality, on flat vectors.
